introduction:
Database normalization is a systematic approach to organizing data in a database to eliminate redundancy 
      and reduce anomalies. It involves decomposing a relation into smaller, well-structured relations based on 
      functional dependencies. Normalization ensures data integrity, reduces storage space, and prevents update, 
      insertion, and deletion anomalies.

sections.title: First Normal Form (1NF)
sections.content:
A relation is in 1NF if every attribute contains only atomic (indivisible) values. There should be no 
        repeating groups or multivalued attributes. Each cell must contain a single value.
sections.code:
Example - Not in 1NF:
Student(student_id, name, courses)
courses: "DBMS, OS, Networks"

In 1NF:
Student(student_id, name)
Enrollment(student_id, course)

sections.title: Second Normal Form (2NF)
sections.content:
A relation is in 2NF if it is in 1NF and every non-key attribute is fully functionally dependent on 
        the primary key. No partial dependencies are allowed.
sections.code:
Example - Not in 2NF:
Order(order_id, product_id, product_name, quantity)
FD: order_id, product_id → quantity
    product_id → product_name (partial dependency)

In 2NF:
Order(order_id, product_id, quantity)
Product(product_id, product_name)

sections.title: Third Normal Form (3NF)
sections.content:
A relation is in 3NF if it is in 2NF and no non-key attribute is transitively dependent on the primary key. 
        All non-key attributes must depend directly on the primary key.
sections.code:
Example - Not in 3NF:
Student(student_id, name, dept_id, dept_name, location)
FD: student_id → dept_id → dept_name, location
    (transitive dependency)

In 3NF:
Student(student_id, name, dept_id)
Department(dept_id, dept_name, location)

sections.title: Boyce-Codd Normal Form (BCNF)
sections.content:
A relation is in BCNF if it is in 3NF and every determinant is a candidate key. BCNF is stricter than 3NF 
        and eliminates all redundancy based on functional dependencies.
sections.code:
Example - Not in BCNF:
Course(course_id, instructor, time, room)
FD: course_id → instructor
    instructor, time → room
    (instructor is not a key)

In BCNF:
Course(course_id, instructor, time)
Room(instructor, time, room)

functionalDependencies.title: Functional Dependencies
functionalDependencies.content:
A functional dependency (FD) is a constraint between two sets of attributes in a relation. If attribute set 
      X functionally determines attribute set Y (denoted as X → Y), then for any two tuples with the same values in X, 
      they must have the same values in Y.
functionalDependencies.code:
Example:
If student_id → name, email
Then: If two tuples have the same student_id, 
      they must have the same name and email.

keyTerms.description: The left side of a functional dependency (X in X → Y)
The right side of a functional dependency (Y in X → Y)
Y is a subset of X (e.g., AB → A)
Y is not a subset of X
Y is functionally dependent on X but not on any proper subset of X
Y is functionally dependent on a proper subset of X

anomalies.title: Database Anomalies
anomalies.content: Unnormalized databases suffer from three types of anomalies that can lead to data inconsistency:

types.name: Insertion Anomaly
types.description: Cannot insert certain information without other information (e.g., cannot add a department without an employee)

types.name: Deletion Anomaly
types.description: Deleting one piece of information causes loss of other information (e.g., deleting last employee deletes department info)

types.name: Update Anomaly
types.description: Changing one value requires updating multiple rows (e.g., updating department name in multiple employee records)

practicalExample.title: Practical Example
practicalExample.description: Consider an unnormalized table: Student_Course(student_id, name, course_id, course_name, grade, instructor)
practicalExample.code:
Functional Dependencies:
student_id → name
course_id → course_name, instructor
student_id, course_id → grade

Normalized to 3NF:
Student(student_id, name)
Course(course_id, course_name, instructor)
Enrollment(student_id, course_id, grade)
